title: Timepoint 3 — Synthesis and management
scenarioBrief: Hospital day 11 (2027-03-25). The inpatient workup is back: muscle biopsy, skin biopsy, MBSS, EMG, serologies, consults. Maria is being discharged today. You are responsible for committing to a unifying diagnosis and writing her discharge plan.

chartGate._notes: TP3 visibility: full chart through hospital day 11 — admission H&P, MBSS, EMG, muscle biopsy, skin biopsy, consult notes, trending labs, daily progress notes, discharge summary.

prompts.question: Commit to a unifying diagnosis (or dyad/triad) for Maria. Lay out your reasoning.

essential: Active systemic lupus erythematosus is the underlying autoimmune diagnosis
Inflammatory myositis is present as part of the SLE picture (or as overlap with dermatomyositis)
Oropharyngeal dysphagia is mechanistically attributed to myositis affecting pharyngeal muscles
Severe protein-calorie malnutrition / kwashiorkor is recognized as a major and consequential co-diagnosis, not a downstream side issue
Explicit causal chain: SLE -> myositis -> dysphagia -> starvation -> kwashiorkor
bonus: Recognizes that earlier presentations (TP1, intercurrent ED visits) were missed opportunities to break this chain
Identifies social-determinant amplifiers (insurance lapse, food access, language) that accelerated the malnutrition
Acknowledges the prognosis is driven as much by nutritional recovery as by SLE control
Recognizes the scaling rash as potentially kwashiorkor-related dermatosis as well as / instead of pure SLE skin
Considers thyroid involvement of the autoimmune process given the long-standing Hashimoto's
redFlags: Treating SLE and malnutrition as two unrelated diagnoses
Missing the malnutrition / kwashiorkor link entirely
Attributing the skin findings purely to SLE without considering kwashiorkor dermatosis
Failing to articulate any causal chain
Suggesting that nutrition is 'secondary' or 'less important than SLE control'

prompts.question: Write Maria's discharge management plan. Address immunosuppression, nutrition, and the outpatient transition.

essential: Specific corticosteroid plan with stated dose and taper (e.g., prednisone 1 mg/kg with slow taper schedule)
Steroid-sparing agent named (mycophenolate or azathioprine) with confirmed pre-treatment infection screen
Slow caloric initiation with explicit refeeding precautions: daily phos/Mg/K monitoring, thiamine repletion
Enteral access OR modified-consistency diet plan tied explicitly to the swallow-study finding
Concrete medication-access plan addressing the insurance barriers (patient-assistance programs, low-cost options)
Language-concordant outpatient follow-up scheduling (Spanish-speaking PCP, rheum, dietitian, or qualified interpreter)
Specific outpatient follow-up plan: PCP, rheumatology, nutrition, SLP, with intervals
bonus: Specific dose rationale tied to severity (e.g., why 1 mg/kg vs pulse)
Contraception counseling for a young woman on mycophenolate (teratogenicity)
Bone health considerations (calcium, vitamin D, bisphosphonate)
Micronutrient repletion (zinc, selenium, vitamin D, B-vitamins)
Warm handoff to community resources (food bank, FQHC, lupus foundation)
Return-precaution / safety-net teaching tied to specific symptoms
PCP prophylaxis with TMP/SMX while on combined immunosuppression
redFlags: Skipping infection screen check prior to confirming immunosuppression escalation
Aggressive caloric initiation (>20 kcal/kg/day from day 1) without refeeding precautions
Oral feeding without addressing dysphagia / aspiration risk
Generic discharge instructions ignoring social-determinant barriers
No medication-access plan despite known insurance issues
Starting mycophenolate without addressing teratogenicity in a young woman
Treating malnutrition as 'will fix itself' once SLE is controlled
